title: Explore
description: You are in the exploration phase. Research the codebase, understand existing patterns, and gather context about the problem space. If you are not certain about conventions or rules, ask the user about them. Read relevant files and documentation. If  exists, understand and document requirements there, otherwise document them in your task management system. Don't write code yet - focus on understanding. Document your findings and create tasks as needed.

title: Plan
description: You are in the planning phase. Create a detailed implementation strategy based on your exploration. If  exists, base your strategy on requirements from , otherwise use existing task context. Break down the work into specific, actionable tasks. Consider edge cases, dependencies, and potential challenges. If it impacts the general architecture and  exists, document this in , otherwise create tasks to track architectural decisions. If  exists, make sure to adhere to the design in . Document the planning work thoroughly and create implementation tasks as needed.

title: Code
description: Follow your plan and if  exists, follow the design from  to build the solution. If  exists, build according to the architecture in . If  exists, ensure requirements from  are met, otherwise ensure existing requirements are met based on your task context. Write clean, well-structured code with proper error handling. Prevent regression by building, linting and executing existing tests. Stay flexible and adapt the plan as you learn more during implementation. Update task progress and create new tasks as needed for implementation work.

title: Phase 1: Create oauth2-proxy namespace
description: Create dedicated oauth2-proxy namespace with proper labels and security policies

title: Phase 3: Create oauth2-callback HTTPRoute
description: Create unprotected HTTPRoute at /oauth2/callback for GitHub OAuth redirect

title: Phase 3: Deploy Phase 3 components and verify
description: Run pulumi up for Phase 3, verify middlewares and callback route created

title: Phase 4: Create example protected HTTPRoute with OAuth2-Proxy
description: Create example-oauth2 app protected by forwardauth-oauth2-users middleware

title: Phase 4: Test unauthenticated access - redirect to GitHub
description: Verify curl to protected route returns 302/303 redirect to GitHub OAuth

title: Phase 4: Test authenticated user in allowlist - access granted
description: Verify authenticated user with email in allowlist can access protected route

title: Phase 4: Test authenticated user NOT in allowlist - 403 Forbidden
description: Verify authenticated user with email NOT in allowlist gets 403 response

title: Phase 4: Test Authelia routes still work
description: Verify existing Authelia-protected routes (auth-demo) still function correctly

title: Phase 4: Test cookie isolation - no conflicts
description: Verify OAuth2-Proxy and Authelia cookies don't interfere with each other

title: Phase 5: Document user onboarding workflow
description: Create documentation on how to add users: email -> groups.ts -> pulumi up workflow

title: Phase 5: Create example - Adding new group and users
description: Document how to create new groups and add email allowlists

title: Phase 1: Create GitHub OAuth Kubernetes Secret
description: Create oauth2-proxy-github Secret in oauth2-proxy namespace with Client-ID, Secret, and Cookie-Secret from Pulumi config

title: Phase 5: Create example - Protecting new route with OAuth2-Proxy
description: Document how to create new HTTPRoutes protected by OAuth2-Proxy middleware

title: Phase 5: Update main README with OAuth2-Proxy section
description: Add OAuth2-Proxy architecture, decision matrix, and usage guide to project README

title: Add AuthType.OAUTH2_PROXY to ExposedWebApp enum and config interfaces
Implement OAuth2-Proxy middleware stack creation in ExposedWebApp
Implement IngressRoute creation for OAuth2-Proxy protected apps
Create redirect service (nginx) for 401 handling in ExposedWebApp
Test ExposedWebApp with AuthType.OAUTH2_PROXY end-to-end
Update ExposedWebApp documentation for OAuth2-Proxy option
Phase 1: Create config/groups.ts with initial groups
description: Create single source of truth for group definitions with 'users' group and github@beimir.net email

title: Phase 1: Create oauth2-proxy infrastructure directory structure
description: Create packages/core/infrastructure/src/oauth2-proxy directory with proper module exports

title: Phase 2: Create email-configmaps.ts module
description: Create ConfigMaps per group with 'restricted_user_access' key containing email allowlist

title: Phase 2: Create oauth2-proxy Helm releases module
description: Deploy one oauth2-proxy Helm instance per group with unique cookie names and email ConfigMap references

title: Phase 2: Deploy Phase 1 & 2 components and verify
description: Run pulumi up for Phase 1&2, verify pods running and services accessible

title: Phase 3: Verify/Update Traefik allowCrossNamespace config
description: Ensure kubernetesCRD.allowCrossNamespace: true is set in Traefik Helm values

title: Phase 3: Create middlewares.ts - Traefik ForwardAuth CRDs
description: Create one Middleware CRD per group pointing to respective oauth2-proxy service with ForwardAuth config

title: Commit
description: You are in the commit phase. This phase ensures code quality and documentation accuracy through systematic cleanup and review. **STEP 1: Code Cleanup** Systematically clean up development artifacts: 1. **Remove Debug Output**: Search for and remove all temporary debug output statements used during development.    Look for language-specific debug output methods (console logging, print statements, debug output functions).    Remove any debugging statements that were added for development purposes.  2. **Review TODO/FIXME Comments**:    - Address each TODO/FIXME comment by either implementing the solution or documenting why it's deferred    - Remove completed TODOs    - Convert remaining TODOs to proper issue tracking if needed  3. **Remove Debugging Code Blocks**:    - Remove temporary debugging code, test code blocks, and commented-out code    - Clean up any experimental code that's no longer needed    - Ensure proper error handling replaces temporary debug logging  **STEP 2: Documentation Review** Review and update documentation to reflect final implementation: 1. **Update Long-Term Memory Documents**: Based on what was actually implemented:    • Update  if requirements changed during development    • Update  if architectural impacts were identified    • Update  if design details were refined or changed 2. **Compare Against Implementation**: Review documentation against actual implemented functionality 3. **Update Changed Sections**: Only modify documentation sections that have functional changes 4. **Remove Development Progress**: Remove references to development iterations, progress notes, and temporary decisions 5. **Focus on Final State**: Ensure documentation describes the final implemented state, not the development process 6. **Ask User to Review Document Updates** **STEP 3: Final Validation** - Run existing tests to ensure cleanup didn't break functionality - Verify documentation accuracy with a final review - Ensure code is ready for production/delivery   Update task progress and mark completed work as you finalize the feature.
